basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-ʿĀdiyāt — “The Chargers” — is a Meccan sūra whose name comes from the chargers (in the sense of horses or camels at full gallop) mentioned in the first verse. Tradition reports several occasions of revelation. In one, the Prophet sent a military expedition under al-Mundhir ibn ʿAmr; news was delayed, the hypocrites said they had all been killed, and the sūra came as a sign. In another, attributed to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib, the oaths rather describe the rush of mounts during the pilgrimage, hurrying from ʿArafāt to Muzdalifa and on to Minā. Both readings are received. The sūra opens with five vivid oaths on the rush of mounts (vv. 1–5), turns in three verses on human ingratitude and excessive love for goods (vv. 6–8), and ends in three verses on the Day when the secret of hearts and of graves shall be brought into the open (vv. 9–11).

versets.commentaire: al-ʿādiyāt (العَادِيَات): the ones who run — feminine plural active participle of ʿadā (عَدَا), to run, to rush. Ḍabḥ (ضَبْح) names the noisy breath of galloping beasts. The oath is striking: God swears by panting horses, by their very breath. Al-Bayḍāwī also reads, in a spiritual key, the eager souls who rush toward God, and whose very breath becomes prayer.

mots.def: wa: oath particle.
ʿādiyāt: feminine plural active participle of ʿadā (to run, to rush); the ones who run — horses or camels in swift course.
ḍabḥ: the noisy, panting breath of galloping beasts; in the accusative as cognate object.

versets.commentaire: al-mūriyāt (المُورِيَات): the ones that strike fire — hooves that beat the rocks send up sparks. Qadḥ (قَدْح) is the act of striking stone to draw a flame. The image is precise and beautiful: the rush is not only swift, it is luminous, it tears flashes from the ground. The oath rises one step.

mots.def: coordination particle: and then; links the successive oaths.
mūriyāt: feminine plural active participle of awrā (to strike fire); the ones whose hooves draw sparks from the rocks.
qadḥ: the act of striking stone to draw fire; in the accusative as object.

versets.commentaire: al-mughīrāt (المُغِيرَات): the ones who charge, who raid — from the verb aghāra (أَغَارَ), to launch an attack. Ṣubḥ (صُبْح): dawn, early morning. In ancient Arabia, dawn was the hour of attack — the enemy still asleep. By another reading, these are the camels of pilgrimage hurrying at dawn on the Day of Sacrifice: the “charge” becomes a movement of devotion.

mots.def: coordination particle: and then.
mughīrāt: feminine plural active participle of aghāra (to charge, to raid).
ṣubḥ: dawn, early morning; adverbial accusative: “at dawn”.

versets.trad: raising thereby a trail of dust,
versets.commentaire: naqʿ (نَقْع): the dust raised. The rush is so swift that the ground rises behind in a cloud. Verses 1–4 thus form a lively cinema: panting course, sparks, raid at the break of day, dust rising. The ear follows the motion.

mots.def: coordination particle.
athāra: to raise, to stir up (dust); feminine plural perfect.
preposition bi- + masc. sing. suffix pronoun: thereby, in that place (the dawn or the mêlée).
naqʿ: dust raised by the feet of the beasts.

versets.trad: penetrating thereby to the centre of the throng,
versets.commentaire: wasaṭna (وَسَطْنَ): they pierced into the centre — at the heart of the enemy troops, or at the heart of the pilgrim gathering (the jamʿ, which also names the pilgrimage at Muzdalifa). The oath ends on this image of full penetration, and turns in the next verse to deliver its answer.

mots.def: coordination particle.
wasaṭa: to be in the middle of, to penetrate to the centre; feminine plural perfect.
preposition bi- + suffix pronoun.
jamʿ: the gathering, the assembled troop, the mêlée of combat; also a name for the pilgrimage at Muzdalifa.

versets.trad: truly mankind is ungrateful to its Lord,
versets.commentaire: After the five oaths, the sūra delivers its heart: man is kanūd (كَنُود) toward his Lord. The word comes from a root that names barren land where nothing grows — ingratitude is that inner ground where the blessings received do not sprout into thanks. Kanūd also names the one who forgets only the favours, and remembers only the misfortunes. A whole moral portrait in one word.

mots.def: emphasis particle: truly, certainly.
insān: the human being, the generic man.
preposition li- + rabb + suffix pronoun: toward his Lord.
affirmative particle.
kanūd: ungrateful; the word also evokes barren land where nothing grows, and the one who only forgets the favours received.

versets.trad: and truly to that he himself is witness;
versets.commentaire: shahīd (شَهِيد): witness. According to some commentators, the “he” of the verse designates God, the universal Witness; according to others, it is man himself, witness of his own ingratitude. The second reading is the most morally striking: man knows, deep down, what he is doing. And on the Day of Judgment, the Quran says that people will bear witness against themselves (cf. 36:65; 41:20).

mots.def: conjunction: and.
inna + suffix pronoun: truly, he.
preposition: upon, regarding.
far masculine demonstrative: that (referring to v. 6).
affirmative particle.
shahīd: witness; for commentators, either God or man himself, witness against himself.

versets.trad: and truly his love for good things is fierce.
versets.commentaire: al-khayr (الخَيْر) means “the good”, and commentators render it here as “good things” — wealth, by metonymy, is named “the good” in Arabic. Man loves what he possesses with a violent affection — shadīd (شَدِيد), “intense”, “fierce”. Elsewhere the Quran names this love in detail: “women, children, hoarded heaps of gold and silver, horses of mark, cattle, tillage” (3:14). The sūra reaches wider than mere greed: every excessive attachment to what passes.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
inna + suffix pronoun.
ḥubb: love, attachment; preposition li- + noun in the genitive.
khayr: the good, what is good; here by extension, material goods, wealth.
affirmative particle.
shadīd: intense, fierce, firm; here describes excessive love.

versets.ar: أَفَلَا يَعْلَمُ إِذَا بُعْثِرَ مَا فِي الْقُبُورِ
versets.tr: a-fa-lā yaʿlamu idhā buʿthira mā fī l-qubūr
versets.trad: Does he not know that when what lies within graves is turned inside out,
versets.commentaire: buʿthira (بُعْثِرَ): to be turned over, overturned, as one turns a vessel upside down. The image is of graves giving up their contents in a movement of full inversion. The Quran often employs this grammar of apocalypse: what was hidden below is set above, what was sleeping is awakened. The verse opens with an indignant question: “Does he then not know…?”

mots.def: interrogative particle.
fa- + lā: not (negation accompanying the question).
ʿalima: to know.
temporal particle: when.
buʿthira: to be overturned, to be turned inside out; passive perfect with future sense.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun.
preposition: in.
qubūr: plural of qabr, the grave.

versets.trad: and what lies within breasts is made known,
versets.commentaire: ḥuṣṣila (حُصِّلَ): to be drawn out, gathered, brought into the open — as ore is drawn from the mine, or as the harvest is gathered. al-ṣudūr (الصُّدُور), “the breasts”, names the inner heart, the inmost self. All that was held secret will be drawn out from it. Elsewhere the Quran names this Day “the Day when secrets are tested” (86:9). More unsettling still than the overturning of graves: the turning out of inner selves.

mots.def: conjunction: and.
ḥaṣṣala: to draw, to gather, to bring out what was hidden; passive perfect, future sense.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: neuter relative pronoun.
preposition.
ṣadr: the breast, the seat of the inmost heart; plural ṣudūr.

versets.trad: on that Day their Lord shall be fully aware of them.
versets.commentaire: khabīr (خَبِير): the One who knows by deep experience, who knows the bottom of things — one of the Divine Names. The verse teaches God nothing: He knows already. What changes on that Day is that men themselves will at last know that God knew; and that new awareness becomes the trial. The sūra, opened on panting horses, closes on the silent knowing of God.

mots.def: emphasis particle.
rabb + plural suffix pronoun: their Lord.
preposition bi- + plural suffix pronoun: regarding them.
yawm + idh: on that day (the Day of Judgment).
affirmative particle.
khabīr: the One who knows by deep experience, who knows the bottom of things; a Divine Name.
